perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information disclosed hereunder shall be disclosed i n written, electronic or other permanent form and shall be prominently identified as confidential using an appropriate legend, marking stamp, or other clear and conspicuous written identification which unambiguously indicates that the information being pro vided is the Disclosing Party 's Confidential Information. If Confidential Information is disclosed in other than written or other permanent form, it shall be considered Confidential Information as of the time of original disclosure if: i) the Disclosing Pa rty identifies it as Confidential Information at the time of the original disclosure and ii) Mutual Non -Disclosure Agre ement Northeastern University within thirty (30) days of the non -written disclosure, the Disclosing Party reduces the information to written or other permanent form, clearly identifying the inf ormation confidential as indicated above, and transmits it to the Recipient , referencing the place and date of such oral disclosure and identifying the name(s) of the employees or officers of the Recipient to whom it was made . The rights and obligations provided by this Agreement shall take precedence over specific legends or statements associated with Confidential Information when received .
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information disclosed hereunder shall be disclosed i n written, electronic or other permanent form. If Confidential Information is disclosed in other than written or other permanent form, it shall be considered Confidential Information as of the time of original disclosure if: i) the Disclosing Pa rty identifies it as Confidential Information at the time of the original disclosure and ii) Mutual Non -Disclosure Agre ement Northeastern University within thirty (30) days of the non -written disclosure, the Disclosing Party reduces the information to written or other permanent form, clearly identifying the inf ormation confidential as indicated above, and transmits it to the Recipient , referencing the place and date of such oral disclosure and identifying the name(s) of the employees or officers of the Recipient to whom it was made . The rights and obligations provided by this Agreement shall take precedence over specific legends or statements associated with Confidential Information when received .
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement that confidential information must be prominently identified, the enforceability of the NDA is weakened. It's now harder to prove what information was intended to be confidential.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification weakens the identification of confidential information, potentially hindering the ability to prove damages or unfair practices, and could be viewed as an unfair or deceptive act under Massachusetts law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 11 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 11 Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 11: Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11. Any person who engages in the conduct of any trade or commerce and who suffers any loss of money or property, real or personal, as a result of the use or employment by another person who engages in any trade or commerce of an unfair method of competition or an unfair or deceptive act or practice declared unlawful by section two or by any rule or regulation issued under paragraph (c) of section two may, as hereinafter provided, bring an action in the superior court, or in the housing court as provided in section three of chapter one hundred and eighty-five C, whether by way of original complaint, counterclaim, cross-claim or third-party action for damages and such equitable relief, including an injunction, as the court deems to be necessary and proper. Such person, if he has not suffered any loss of money or property, may obtain such an injunction if it can be shown that the aforementioned unfair method of competition, act or practice may have the effect of causing such loss of money or property. Any persons entitled to bring such action may, if the use or employment of the unfair method of competition or the unfair or deceptive act or practice has caused similar injury to numerous other persons similarly situated and if the court finds in a preliminary hearing that he adequately and fairly represents such other persons, bring the action on behalf of himself and such other similarly injured and

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for confidential information to be prominently identified, which directly undermines the ability to prove damages or unfair practices related to confidential information. The Law Explanation suggests this could be viewed as an unfair or deceptive act under Massachusetts law, and while the provided snippets don't explicitly define what constitutes an "unfair or deceptive act," they establish the basis for actions against such practices (Snippet 1 and 2: "Any person who engages in the conduct of any trade or commerce and who suffers any loss of money or property, real or personal, as a result of the use or employment by another person who engages in any trade or commerce of an unfair method of competition or an unfair or deceptive act or practice declared unlawful by section two..."). Removing the identification requirement makes it significantly harder to enforce confidentiality, thus contradicting the intent to protect businesses from unfair trade practices involving confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: Should the Recipient become aware of any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement, the Recipient agrees to promptly notify the Disclosing Party in writing and , in addition to the other rights set forth in this Agreement, the Disclosing Party shall have the right to immediately terminate this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Should the Recipient become aware of any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement, the Recipient agrees to notify the Disclosing Party, but the timing and means of that notification can be determined at the discretion of the Recipient and , in addition to the other rights set forth in this Agreement, the Disclosing Party shall have the right to immediately terminate this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'but the timing and means of that notification can be determined at the discretion of the Recipient', the duty to promptly notify is undermined. This reduces the enforceability of the notification clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaying or hindering the notification process when a breach occurs can be construed as an unfair act or practice under Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93A, because it impedes the disclosing party's ability to mitigate damages or take corrective action.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. perturbation.justification: The changed text undermines the 'promptly notify' obligation by granting the Recipient discretion over the timing and means of notification, which contradicts the duty to provide timely notification of a breach, potentially harming the Disclosing Party's ability to mitigate damages. The Law Explanation states that delaying notification can be construed as an unfair act under Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93A. While the scraped snippets do not explicitly state 'prompt notification' requirements, they broadly prohibit 'unfair or deceptive acts or practices' (Snippet 1 & 2, Section 2(a)). Allowing the recipient to delay notification introduces a scenario where the disclosing party is unfairly disadvantaged due to delayed information, potentially classifying this as an unfair practice under Chapter 93A.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties hereby e xpressly consent to the exclusive personal jurisdiction of the state and federal courts located in the Commonwealth of Massachusetts for any dispute arising from or relating to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties hereby consent to the personal jurisdiction of courts for any dispute arising from or relating to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'exclusive' and 'the state and federal courts located in the Commonwealth of Massachusetts' this could allow disputes to be heard in any jurisdiction, contradicting the original intent of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Rule 12(h)
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the specification of exclusive jurisdiction, the parties risk inadvertently waiving their right to object to jurisdiction in an inconvenient forum, which conflicts with the intent to define a specific jurisdiction for disputes.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 12. Defenses and Objections: When and How Presented; Motion for Judgment on the Pleadings; Consolidating Motions; Waiving Defenses; Pretrial Hearing | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 12. Defenses and Objections: When and How Presented; Motion for Judgment on the Pleadings; Consolidating Motions; Waiving Defenses; Pretrial Hearing (a) Time to Serve a Responsive Pleading. Unless another time is specified by a federal statute, the time for serving a responsive pleading is as follows: (1) In General. (A) A defendant must serve an answer: (i) within 21 days after being served with the summons and complaint; or (ii) if it has timely waived service under Rule 4(d) , within 60 days after the request for a waiver was sent, or within 90 days after it was sent to the defendant outside any judicial district of the United States. (B) A party must serve an answer to a counterclaim or crossclaim within 21 days after being served with the pleading that states the counterclaim or crossclaim. (C) A party must serve a reply to an answer within 21 days after being served with an order to reply, unless the order specifies a different time. (2) United States and Its Agencies, Officers, or Employees Sued in an Official Capacity. The United States, a United States agency, or a United States officer or employee sued only in an official capacity must serve an answer to a complaint, counterclaim, or crossclaim within 60 days after service on the United States attorney. (3) United States Officers or Employees Sued in an Individual Capacity. A United States officer or employee sued in an individual capacity for an act or omission occurring in connection with duties performed on the United States’ behalf must serve an answer to a complaint, counterclaim, or crossclaim within 60 days after service on the officer or employee or service on the United States attorney, whichever is later. (4) Effect of a
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies 'exclusive personal jurisdiction' in Massachusetts, whereas the changed text removes 'exclusive' and 'the state and federal courts located in the Commonwealth of Massachusetts', broadening the potential jurisdiction for disputes. This contradicts the original intent, as noted in the Perturbation Explanation. The Law Explanation further supports this by stating that the parties risk inadvertently waiving their right to object to jurisdiction in an inconvenient forum, which is against the intent to define a specific jurisdiction for disputes. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Rule 12, discusses waiving defenses. While Snippet 1 doesn't directly address exclusive jurisdiction, the removal of the exclusive term could allow for a waiver of the right to object to jurisdiction elsewhere, making the contradiction significant, as it directly alters the scope of jurisdiction initially agreed upon, and thus, falls under structural flaws legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Company shall not provide any export controlled information , materials , technology to NU, in any form, without prior written notice to NU of Company ’s desire to share export controlled information, and written consent from NU to accept s uch information. Notice to the University shall include the proper export classification and a reference the applicable export control laws and regulations. Company shall indemnify and hold harmless NU and its faculty, employees, student, agents and repre sentatives against any and all claims, damages, losses, or costs arising out of Company ’s breach of this Section 7.
perturbation.changed_text: Company shall not provide any export controlled information , materials , technology to NU, in any form, without prior written notice to NU of Company ’s desire to share export controlled information, and written consent from NU to accept s uch information. Company shall indemnify and hold harmless NU and its faculty, employees, student, agents and repre sentatives against any and all claims, damages, losses, or costs arising out of Company ’s breach of this Section 7.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause that states, 'Notice to the University shall include the proper export classification and a reference the applicable export control laws and regulations.' this creates a gap of information which weakens NU's awareness of risk regarding export compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: By omitting the requirement for proper export classification and reference to applicable export control laws, the agreement may fail to adhere to the due diligence requirements outlined in the Export Administration Regulations (EAR), specifically regarding the "Know Your Customer" guidance, potentially leading to violations of export control laws.

Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates that the company provide the university with the 'proper export classification and a reference the applicable export control laws and regulations' when sharing export-controlled information. The changed text removes this requirement. This omission weakens NU's ability to comply with export regulations because they are not automatically informed of the relevant classifications and regulations. The Law Explanation suggests this might violate 'Know Your Customer' due diligence requirements under the Export Administration Regulations (EAR). Scraped Law Snippet 2 states the EAR helps you navigate export regulations under 15 CFR 730-774. The omission creates a structural flaw by hindering NU's ability to manage export compliance risks effectively.
